On your next turn you could then capture one of them, like the b2-pawn, which is very hard for them to defend. This turns your active rook into a real threat and gains you material.

Takeaway: Before giving a check, see if you can first attack something the opponent can't easily defend.

I'd play Bf5. As an intermediate player, you now know that a bishop belongs on a strong diagonal where it attacks and defends key squares—here, Bf5 eyes your opponent's loose knight and kingside pawns while defending your h7 pawn. Your move f5 weakened control of f5, so let’s use the bishop to take that square ourselves, improving a piece and building pressure. Takeaway: When you have the option, place your bishop where it attacks something and defends a weak point in one move.

I'd play Qe3.  
Pushing your pawn to f4 is tempting to kick the knight, but Qe3 adds much more by also threatening Black's weak pawn on b6 and strongly defending your e4 pawn. That way you develop an attack while securing your center, which is a key habit to learn.  
Takeaway: When you can, choose a queen move that creates multiple threats and supports your central pawns at the same time.

I'd play Bf5. Your pawn move f5 gave up control of the e6-square, so the principled improvement is to reposition your bishop to a much more active diagonal where it targets the pinned knight on b1 and also eyes the weakened h3-pawn. This develops your last minor piece with tempo, forcing White to attend to the threat against the knight, and then you can follow up by doubling rooks on the d-file to increase pressure on their queen. Takeaway: After your opponent pushes a pawn, look for ways to reposition your pieces onto new, more aggressive lines.

I'd play Re2.

Your check is okay, but it's a bit too slow — White can just step the king aside and keep his knight active. Instead, you should use your rook to attack two of his weak pawns on the queenside, putting immediate pressure on b2 and g2 while your bishop still covers d6. This forces White to decide how to defend both pawns, and often leads to grabbing one of them cleanly. In advanced positions, creating threats against multiple weaknesses is stronger than a one-time check that can be easily sidestepped.

Takeaway: A rook landing on the second rank often attacks multiple pawns and creates more lasting problems than a check that the king can simply walk away from.

I'd play Bf5. Your move f5 allowed your pawn to become pinned, which is why I recommend bringing your bishop to a square where it attacks two loose white pieces—the knight on b1 and the pawn on h3—while also safely defending your own h7 pawn. The idea is simple: create multiple small threats that are easy for your opponent to overlook. This is better than moving a pawn that could get stuck.

Takeaway: Before moving a pawn in front of your king, look for ways to use your pieces to harass the opponent's undefended pieces.

I'd play b4. That pawn move is an excellent chance for you to take the initiative by attacking White's backward pawn on c3, creating a target they have to defend. Your move Kf8 was safe, but active play like this helps you make threats instead of just waiting. Try to open lines for your rook while keeping your king safe for now.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a weak pawn, attacking it is often stronger than a quiet king move.

I'd play Qe3.

Your move f4 is fine because it pushes the knight, but Qe3 does that while also hitting b6 and protecting e4 — it's a classic multi-purpose queen move at a moment when Black's pieces are awkward. This way you keep pressure on the weakened queenside pawns without leaving your own center vulnerable. Takeaway: When you have an active queen, look for squares where she defends your weaknesses while attacking two of the opponent’s pieces or pawns.

I'd play Qe3.  
Your f4 move is sensible because it kicks the knight and wins space, but you missed a chance to put your queen on a more flexible central square where it both attacks Black’s weak pawn on b6 and secures your e4 pawn. By moving to e3, you also cover your rook on c1, which might become important if Black tries to attack it later. This keeps your pieces safe while building pressure on both sides of the board.  
Takeaway: When you have a strong center, look for queen moves that defend your key pawns and create multiple threats at once.

I'd play b4.  
Your king move wasn't necessary yet; Black is very solid here, and a good plan is to attack White's center by pushing your queenside pawns. The move b4 threatens to win a pawn by taking on c3 and then on d4, giving you a strong passed pawn. First create this threat, and only worry about bringing your king to safety if White creates a threat of their own.  
Takeaway: When you're safe, look to improve your position with active pawn advances before making a quiet king move.

I'd play b4.

This move takes the initiative by attacking the pawn on c3 right away, creating immediate pressure instead of making a quieter king move. Pawn breaks like this are key to unlocking a cramped position, and here it begins to challenge White's center and fight for space on the queenside. Your move Kf8 is safe but passive; advancing the b-pawn is a more active way to start improving your position.

Takeaway: When your pieces are coordinated, look for a timely pawn break to challenge your opponent's center and create targets.

I'd play Bd2.  
Your knight move was natural, but right now your e3-pawn needs protection and your bishop on c1 is the only piece not doing any work—bringing it to d2 puts it on a useful diagonal while covering that weak pawn. This simple "develop and defend" idea keeps your position solid and prepares to possibly expand in the center next.  
Takeaway: When a pawn is loose, look to defend it with a developing move that improves your worst-placed piece.

I'd play Bd2.  
Your move did protect the e3-pawn, but it left your bishop on c1 undeveloped. Bd2 develops your last minor piece to a safer square, keeps the e3-pawn defended, and connects your rooks. It’s a simple, strong move that follows the opening principles you already know.  
Takeaway: When your king is safe, look to develop your remaining pieces before making another move with one you’ve already moved.

I'd play b5. Your move was safe and keeps your rooks connected, but here we can be more active by striking in the center and opening files for your rooks. Pushing this pawn attacks two of Black's pawns at once, and if they capture, you can recapture with your c-pawn to gain space and activity. Takeaway: Before moving a piece, look for a pawn move that creates threats and improves your position.

I'd play Bd2.  
This develops your last minor piece to a safe square and solidly defends the e3 pawn, putting your position in good order before pursuing a plan. The move you chose, Nd5, is solid too, but it blocks your central pawns and leaves your bishop on e2 inactive; by playing Bd2 first, you keep the knight's flexibility and can follow up with a timely push of your f-pawn to attack on the kingside.  
Takeaway: When your position is secure, finish your development and connect your pieces before committing your knights to fixed central posts.

I'd play b5.  
Your rook move was safe but passes up a chance: pushing b5 strikes at Black's queenside pawns and gains space, which fits the intermediate theme of making your pawn majority work for you. Then after a likely pawn capture you can recapture and challenge their defenders with your rook on the open file.  
Takeaway: When you have a queenside pawn majority, look for a timely advance to create pressure and open lines for your rooks.

I'd play Nbd2. Your knight move tried to prepare play in the center, but it left your queen undefended and didn't develop a new piece. Instead, bring your final knight into the game to support your other knight and rook, which builds a solid foundation before starting an attack. Then you'll be ready to challenge Black's exposed queen with your bishop next. Takeaway: Develop all your pieces before starting complications.

I'd play b5.  
You made a solid, safe choice with Rab1 defending your b4 pawn, but here you have a chance to seize the initiative by pushing b5 to challenge Black's queenside pawns and create an immediate threat. This puts pressure on both a6 and c6, forcing Black to react and giving you a clearer plan to expand your space advantage and maybe open lines for your rooks.  
Takeaway: Look for active pawn breaks in the center or on the flank to give your pieces more scope.

I'd play Nbd2.

Your move develops a piece and covers some squares, but right now simply developing your last minor piece to a safe central square is the clearest improvement. Putting the knight on d2 connects your rooks and prepares to support your other knight, which can then look for ideas like moving to c4 to pressure Black's position. This keeps your position flexible and solid, which is especially good when you're still figuring out the best plan.

Takeaway: When there's no immediate tactical shot, finish your development to get all your pieces working together.

I'd play Bb5.  
Your idea of protecting your f2 pawn was a good start, but you missed an even stronger basic principle: develop and attack at the same time. With Bb5, you bring your bishop into the game and immediately attack the knight that defends Black's central pawn.  
This puts immediate pressure on Black's position and makes it harder for them to get their king safe, giving you the initiative.  
Takeaway: Look for developing moves that also create a direct threat.

I'd play Nbd2.

Your Ne5 wasn't a blunder, but it's a bit premature; you're thinking about controlling the center and covering your queen, but right now getting all your pieces into the game is the top priority. By developing your knight to d2, you complete your minor piece development, solidly protect your knight on f3, and your rook on f1, and you're ready to follow up with Ba3 to attack the black queen. This simple, strong sequence increases your activity while challenging your opponent's best-placed piece.

Takeaway: When you've castled, focus on finishing development and connecting your rooks before making central advances.

I'd play Ng5. You chose a good move in Bg3—it defended weak pawns and avoided any immediate trouble—but Ng5 is more active because it attacks two key black pieces, which forces your opponent to react and creates chances. Then if they take your pawn, you can win their bishop and unbalance the position in your favor. Takeaway: When you're safe, look for moves that attack more than one target to create problems for your opponent.

I'd play Ng5.  
You protected your pawn on f2, but here you can seize the initiative by attacking the bishop on e6 and threatening to take on f7, which would expose the black king and give you strong pressure instead of playing passively.  
With the knight on g5 you also create a double attack on h7, giving your rook a future target and making it hard for black to defend everything comfortably.  

Takeaway: When your pieces are well developed, look for active moves that create threats against your opponent's weak spots rather than just defending your own.

I'd play Rxc6+.  
Your move b4 is reasonable, but here you have a chance to make a more forcing move that opens up the Black king. Capturing on c6 with check disrupts their defenses, and after they take back with the pawn, your queen can jump in to a6 with a strong attack on their rook and bishop. This is a good habit: look for checks and captures that create immediate threats.  
Takeaway: When the enemy king is stuck in the center, look for exchanges that open lines for your heavy pieces.

I'd play Rxc6+.

Your pawn move b4 is solid, but it missed a chance to immediately put the black king in trouble. Capturing the knight with check reveals that the queen on e6 and bishop on b6 are both on the same diagonal as your rook on c1, which can win material after they recapture. Look for checks that uncover attacks from your other pieces. Takeaway: A check can be strongest when it forces a recapture that leaves your other pieces powerfully aligned.

I'd play Bg2.  
It's a safe, straightforward move that gets your bishop to a good square where it helps control the center and defends your rook. From there, a clear plan is to castle kingside, bringing your king to safety and connecting your rooks. This improves your position and builds a foundation for your next moves.  
Takeaway: When you're ahead in development, prioritize castling and coordinating your pieces.

I'd play Rfd1.  
Your b4 move doesn't address the central tension — the pawn on d4 restricts your pieces and blocks your bishop. Bringing a rook to d1 attacks that key pawn immediately, and more importantly prepares to challenge Black's central control by supporting e3 next move. This opens the diagonal for your bishop on g2 and gets your final piece into the game.  

Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong central pawn, contest it directly by developing your last rook to a file where it can apply pressure.

I'd play Bf3.

Right now your bishop on e4 is loose, so just moving it away is wise. Bf3 shifts it to a safe square where it not only protects your e2 pawn but also eyes the c6 pawn, giving Black a small weakness to think about. This is a calm, solid move that lets you finish your development comfortably before starting an attack. Takeaway: When a piece is undefended, reposition it so it still does a useful job like defending a key pawn or controlling a weak square.

I'd play Bg2.

Your knight move wasn't a mistake—it's actually strong—but it shows you're looking for tactics right away, which is great. At this level, a stronger habit is to first improve your least active piece while keeping your options open; here, that's your unmoved bishop. Bg2 develops it to a natural square, connects your rooks, prepares to castle, and keeps pressure on c6 while defending your rook. This sets up your strong knight to move later with more support. Takeaway: When you have a promising tactical idea, first secure your position with development so you can launch it with maximum effect.

I'd play Bg5.  
Your opponent's queen on d8 is a key piece that isn't well-protected; moving your bishop to g5 attacks it directly and forces black to lose time moving it, while also adding a defender to your own queen and h-pawn.  
Castling queenside left your king somewhat exposed on that side of the board, so a simpler and more forcing move like this is a better idea to improve your position and create a threat.  
Takeaway: Look for opportunities to attack your opponent's most valuable pieces while improving the safety of your own.

I'd play Bg5.  
You were thinking about king safety, but queenside castling walked right into ...b4, kicking your knight and opening lines against your king. Instead, first put your bishop on g5 to pin the queen and challenge Black's strong dark-squared bishop—this simple pin adds pressure, protects your queen and h-pawn, and keeps your knight on c3 safe from ...b4 for a move longer.  
Takeaway: When your knight is supporting a center pawn, a pin can often defend it indirectly while developing your own initiative.

I'd play Bg5. This move develops a piece while attacking the opponent's queen, forcing it to move, and it also adds protection to your own queen on d2. After the queen moves, you can follow with a3 to solidify your queenside pawns and prepare to expand, which is a more solid plan than castling long into a potential attack. You correctly wanted to get your king safe, but here it's better to make a useful move first and see where Black's pieces go.

Takeaway: When you can develop with tempo by attacking the opponent's queen, it's often a strong and straightforward way to improve your position.

I'd play c5.

Instead of checking right away, push your pawn to attack Black's weak pawn on d6—this actively creates a threat he must deal with. It keeps your rooks connected and safe for now, preparing for a better check next move. You saw the idea of activating your rook with a check, and here you get the same activity but with an extra threat first. Takeaway: Before moving a piece, look for a pawn push that creates a threat and improves your position.

I'd play c5.

Your move was perfectly fine, but here you can grab the initiative by attacking the pawn on d6, which is the key support for Black's center and knight. If Black takes your pawn, you'll capture back with your rook, opening the d-file toward their king while also threatening their rook on h2. This direct, aggressive pawn push creates more practical problems and forces Black to react immediately. Takeaway: When you have a strong central presence, look for pawn breaks that open lines toward the opponent's king.

I'd play Qc2.

Your move missed a chance to improve your queen’s position safely while preparing a good plan. Instead of moving the bishop, putting your queen on c2 stresses the black king and defends your bishop and c3 pawn, which lets you next move your rooks to the central files. This keeps your pieces working together and avoids any awkward tangles. Takeaway: Move your queen to a square where it attacks and defends something at the same time.

I'd play c5.  
Yes, your check was okay, but here you have a chance to go after Black's weak pawn on d6 and open the center while your rook on d1 is already lined up on the file. Pushing c5 threatens to win that pawn and creates immediate pressure instead of just giving a check Black can easily block.  
Takeaway: When you have a rook on a half-open file, look to attack the weak pawn in front of it.

I'd play Qc2.

That move does two important things at once: it defends your c3 pawn so Black can't push c2 and disrupt your position later, and it eyes a very sensitive diagonal toward the black king, putting some pressure on h7. Your choice of Bf3 is okay, but it just trades pieces and lets Black coordinate easily, while Qc2 keeps your bishop active and builds toward a plan of doubling rooks on the d-file.  
Takeaway: Look for moves that both improve your piece's activity and shore up a potential weakness.

I'd play Qc2.

Your move was fine, but you missed a chance to quickly focus on a threat against the black king by preparing to double rooks on the d-file after the queen steps off. Qc2 defends your pawn and bishop, but more importantly, it eyes the weak h7 square and clears the d1 square for your rook. The immediate idea is to follow with Rfd1, forcing the black queen to move and then piling your rooks on the central file. This kind of proactive queen move, creating multiple threats while improving your structure, is a key step in building an initiative.

Takeaway: Improve your most passive piece to a square where it defends, creates threats, and opens lines for your other pieces.

I'd play Qf3. That centralizes your queen safely while attacking Black's weak pawn on f6 and defending your own e4 pawn, which is more active and useful than retreating your bishop. From f3, your queen eyeing the f6 pawn sets up a simple threat your beginner opponent has to address, and it keeps all your pieces connected. Takeaway: Before retreating a piece, first look for a move that both attacks something and defends your own key squares.

I'd play c4.

Your move Rxf8 was natural — you eliminate a knight on your back rank — but it lets the opponent step their queen into c4 with power. I'd play Bd3. Your move Be2 was safe, but it missed the chance to develop your bishop more actively, where it defends your e4 pawn and clears the f1 square for a possible rook lift. By putting the bishop on d3, you keep pressure toward the center and make it easier to later swing your queen to f3 or f5 to attack Black's weak f6 pawn. This improves a piece while reinforcing your central stronghold. Takeaway: When you have a safe position, develop your pieces to squares where they defend your own weaknesses and point toward targets.

I'd play Bd3.  
You had a good instinct to move your bishop off the back rank, but Be2 doesn’t do much active work for your position. Bd3 is stronger because it develops a piece to a more useful square, where it helps control the center and prepares to support your queen coming to f3 or f4 next. This keeps your initiative and prevents Black from easily advancing their pawns.  
Takeaway: When developing pieces, aim for squares where they have the most scope and support your central plans.

I'd play Bd3+.

Bringing your bishop to d3 attacks the black king, forcing it to move and giving you time to castle safely next. Right now moving your queen first lets Black counterattack your other pieces, but with a check you seize the initiative and get your king safe. This is a better first step than trying to do something complicated in the center.

Takeaway: When you're ahead in development, a simple check can create a threat and give you time to finish castling.

I'd play Bf3. Your idea of targeting the queen wasn't wrong, but your queen is too valuable to trade off early; instead, let's use our bishop to attack the rook on a8 while also defending our queen and king's position. This active move develops with a threat, gains time, and sets up the idea of following with Ra3 to capture their knight. Takeaway: In open positions, use your minor pieces to apply pressure before committing your queen.

I'd play Bf3.

Your move Qd6 was a natural centralizing idea, but here it gives Black time to escape pressure on the queenside. Bf3 immediately attacks the undefended rook in the corner while also adding essential protection to your queen and g-pawn; this simple developing move increases control of the center and makes it harder for Black to coordinate. After Bf3, Black's bishop is practically forced to block on c6, when you can capture and further open lines toward their king.

Takeaway: Before centralizing your queen, look to improve your most passive piece—it often creates the most lasting initiative.

I'd play Bxd3. Your check was okay, but you missed a chance to remove the bishop that's helping defend White's king and threatening your queen. By capturing the bishop first, you trade a minor piece while bringing your queen into attack range with a discovered check on the next move. This forces White's king into the open and keeps the initiative. Takeaway: Look for forcing captures that develop your attack before you give a check that can be easily blocked.

I'd play Bxd3.

Your check is not bad, but it lets White block with their bishop, and you miss a chance to remove their most active piece and trade into a simpler position. Instead, capture that bishop on d3, and when their queen recaptures, you can follow up with a check of your own, exposing their king and also attacking their b2 pawn. This wins a bishop and keeps your queen in a much stronger position. Takeaway: When you can trade a less active piece for your opponent's well-placed defender, take it and look for the next check or threat.

I'd play Kg8.

This is a safe, simple way to bring your king away from the center and onto a safer square, which helps connect your rooks and protects your f-pawn. Your move gave up a knight and gave White free trades, but here you can calmly develop your pieces and build a solid position. The idea is to find a safe spot for your king first, then bring your bishop into the game on g6.

Takeaway: When your king is exposed in the center, prioritize tucking it to safety before grabbing for material.

I'd play Bxd3. You correctly looked for a check, but Qb4+ allows White to block and then reposition his queen favorably. Instead, take the bishop first to remove a key defender. After Qxd3 you can still give the same check, but now your queen will be much safer and you win a useful piece, fixing the structure in your favor. Takeaway: When you have a forcing sequence, sometimes capturing a defender first makes your follow-up much stronger.

I'd play g5.  
You saw a chance to win a bishop, but right now your knight on f4 is loose and could get chased away; pushing the pawn secures that key piece and keeps your kingside safe. This simple move is about protecting what you have before grabbing something, which is especially important when your king is still in the center. Takeaway: When you have an active but undefended piece, look for a quiet move that secures it and shores up your position.

I'd play g5.  
This move secures your knight on f4, which is a key attacker and defender in the center, and it stops White from simply pushing that knight away with h3.  
Your move of taking on e2 unnecessarily traded a strong, active piece, while g5 keeps all your pieces working together and prepares ideas like ...Rg8 next, building kingside pressure.  
Takeaway: Before trading, see if you can first improve a key attacker's stability to maintain the initiative.

I'd play Rxd5.

You noticed your rook on d4 is attacked, but moving it away trades a strong, active rook for a bishop. Instead, capture the bishop on d5 with your rook — this is a strong trade that removes Black’s good defender and then attacks three of their pawns. Your other rook will recapture, keeping the initiative and simplifying the position in your favor. Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, sometimes the best defense is a counterattack that trades favorably.

I'd play Rxd5.

Your move retreated the rook, but Black’s bishop is loose on d5 and your other rook can support the attack. Capturing it with the rook removes a strong defender, attacks multiple pawns, and makes Black work to recover the piece. Then their queen is stuck on d5, blocking their own rook, and you can pile on with your other rook to keep initiative. Takeaway: When you see an undefended piece, look for ways to capture it while developing more threats.

I'd play Rxd5.

The rook on d4 is hanging, and retreating it gives up the initiative; instead, sacrifice it to remove Black’s strong central bishop and expose the weak pawns on b5, d6, and f5. After their queen captures your rook, you can play Rd1 with tempo, gaining time to develop a threat against the queen while activating your rook. This turns a passive retreat into a dynamic counterattack that exploits undeveloped opponents.

Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, look for a forcing capture that creates more threats instead of just moving it away.

I'd play Qc1.

Your instinct to defend your hanging c3 pawn with your rook is reasonable, but it misses a chance to counterattack with a stronger piece. Instead, by moving your queen to c1, you hit two birds with one stone: you attack your opponent's dangerous queen and you add an extra defender to your weak pawn. This forces their queen to move and keeps your pieces active and safe. Takeaway: When you are attacked, look first for a counterattacking defense that threatens something of your opponent's.

I'd play Qc1.

Rc1 is too passive—you're just defending the pawn but letting Black's queen keep all its threats. Instead, your priority should be to challenge her directly and reduce her activity. Moving your queen to c1 attacks hers, putting her on the defensive, and also protects your rook and that pawn on c3. After she moves away, you can follow up with Rb1 to keep the initiative.

Takeaway: When your opponent has an annoying piece, look for a move that trades it or forces it to retreat.

I'd play Bc5. This develops your bishop to a more active square where it attacks two valuable targets: their rook on f8 and the pawn on a7, while also supporting your advanced pawn on a3. It improves your position without the risk of your bishop being kicked away immediately, unlike Bb5 which allowed ...e5 attacking your rook's support. The idea is to build pressure before looking for bigger threats.

Takeaway: When choosing a developing move, pick squares where your piece can’t easily be attacked and gains opportunities to attack.

I'd play Bc5. You developed your bishop aggressively, but here the best idea is to target Black's weak pawn on a7 and create pressure on the long diagonal. The move you played lets Black easily block with ...e5, while Bc5 gives you a simple plan of doubling your rooks on the d-file next, increasing your control. Takeaway: When you have a rook already on an open file, look for ways to activate your other pieces to support that file's pressure.

I'd play Rb1.

You saw the threat to your c3 pawn and wanted to guard it with Rc1, but that leaves your rook passive and doesn't challenge Black's active queen. Moving Rb1 directly attacks the queen, forcing it to decide where to go, and by connecting your rooks you also protect your own queen on d1. This is a more active way to solve the problem, using the principle that when your opponent has a piece on a dangerous square, you should look for ways to harass it while improving your own position.

Takeaway: When your opponent's queen is deep in your territory, look for a move that attacks it while developing another piece.

I'd play g6. While developing a knight with Nc6 is natural, right now you must stop the immediate check from the queen by blocking the diagonal to g8, which is far more urgent. After g6, your king can comfortably go to e7, unpinning the rook and safely giving your king some protection, limiting the opponent's attacking chances. Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is deep in your territory, your first priority is stopping direct checks to secure your king.

I'd play g6. This move kicks the strong white queen away from your kingside, taking away her threats near your king. By pushing the pawn, you force her to move and then you can safely bring your king out next turn. Your move Nc6 was a natural developing idea, but letting her check first causes trouble which we can prevent by simply chasing her off now. Takeaway: When your opponent has a queen near your king, look first for moves that push it away safely.

I'd play Bc5.

Your move developed the bishop to attack the knight, which is a common and good idea, but here you missed a chance to immediately step onto a more aggressive diagonal where it threatens the rook and targets the weak a7-pawn. Bc5 puts immediate pressure on Black and keeps the option to reposition later. By placing the bishop here first, you create more problems for your opponent than simply attacking the knight does. Takeaway: Before developing a piece to an active square, check if there's an even stronger post where it eyes multiple weaknesses.

I'd play g6.

Your move Nc6 develops a knight and shields the king, but it ignores the concrete threat of a discovered check. Here, pushing g6 decisively blocks the queen's dangerous check and prepares a safe square for your king on e7, neutralizing White's main tactical idea. It forces the queen to retreat immediately and secures your position.

Takeaway: When your king is exposed, prioritize moves that cut off checks and secure a safe haven over routine development.

I'd play f6.

You thought about checking the king with your queen, but we should first attack the advanced white pawn on e5 to challenge their center and open lines for your rooks. The move f6 puts immediate pressure on that pawn, and after they defend it with h4, you can capture and gain space. This is a more fundamental idea for beginners: attack what your opponent has advanced before rushing forward with your own queen. Takeaway: Challenge your opponent's central pawns before starting your own attack.

I'd play f6.

Your check was tempting, but you missed a stronger idea: first undermine his center by attacking his e5 pawn. After he defends it, you can use that moment to create threats against his king with your queen, since his king is awkwardly placed on f3. This way you're combining a solid pawn attack with building your own initiative, rather than letting him consolidate after the check. Takeaway: When the opponent's king is exposed, often the best follow-up is to first attack a key central pawn to disrupt his coordination.

I'd play f6.  
Your check is okay, but it lets the king step to safety; instead, first challenge his strong central pawn with f6 to weaken his control.  
This creates immediate pressure, opens the f-file for your rook, and keeps your queen flexible for a follow-up attack.  
Takeaway: When you have an attacking position, often it's stronger to improve your threats against key weaknesses before giving a check the opponent can easily parry.

I'd play Rxd1+.  
Your move Nd5 attacked the queen but let the rook on d1 stay active, which lets White coordinate their pieces. Instead, first trade rooks with check to deflect their rook and then you can go after their loose knight next. This simple capture fixes the mistake by using a check to force the game into a clearer position where your pieces become stronger.  
Takeaway: When your opponent has an active rook, often the first move should be to swap it off — especially with check.

I'd play Rxd1+.  
You grabbed my knight rather than dealing with the check threat; now I can simplify into an endgame where I’m up a pawn. After capturing your rook and then your rook recaptures, I bring my other rook to e8, and suddenly my pieces are active while your queen is awkwardly placed. This way we trade down toward a winning position without letting your queen coordinate with your rooks.  
Takeaway: When you can, cash in a check to force favorable trades and improve your piece activity.

I'd play Rxd1+.

Black is behind in development and needs to resolve the central tension decisively; your move Nd5 missed a chance to exploit White's cramped king and loose pieces. Capturing the rook first forces White's king or remaining rook into an awkward spot, gaining time to then attack the white queen with ...Re8, which also activates your last inactive piece. This sequence of trading to create a threat is a powerful pattern to turn equal material into a winning initiative.

Takeaway: When your opponent's king is exposed, look for forceful moves that give checks and attack their most active defender.

I'd play Bf5. Let's focus on using your pieces actively: your bishop on g4 isn't doing much right now, so moving it to f5 creates an immediate threat against the white rook on d3 while also making your position more solid. Your move Kg8 missed this chance to attack and improve a piece in one go. By moving the bishop, you challenge White right away and get your rook on c8 out of danger.

Takeaway: When you have a quiet move, look for one that improves your piece and creates a threat at the same time.

I'd play Bf5.

Your king move missed the chance to improve your pieces and attack their rook. Bf5 immediately puts your bishop on a strong diagonal where it attacks their rook on d3 and also defends your own rook on c8 and pawn on g6, making your position more solid. This is a practical move that creates threats while also solving some of your own weaknesses.

Takeaway: When you have a chance, use your pieces to attack your opponent's loose pieces and defend your own at the same time.

I'd play Rh4.  
That rook move stays active while also protecting your f4 pawn, which is a useful extra defense. It keeps your rook ready to slide back to a safer square like h1 next if needed. The move you chose, Rh2, didn’t protect anything and left your rook still a bit exposed on the same file.  
Takeaway: When moving a rook, try to make it defend something you care about.

I'd play Bf5. Your move gave away your chance to put immediate pressure on White's rook while also connecting your rooks and defending key squares. Instead, seize the open diagonal to attack the rook on d3—it will likely retreat, and then you can reposition your bishop to maintain the pin and keep your position solid. Takeaway: Look for moves that develop pressure on your opponent’s active pieces while improving the coordination of your own.

I'd play Rh4.  
Rh2 leaves your rook a bit passive and doesn't defend anything actively; instead, Rh4 makes a useful double job of securing your f4-pawn and staying connected with your other pieces. This frees your dark bishop to move without dropping the pawn, and next move you could consider bringing the rook back toward the center like Rh1.  
Takeaway: When moving a rook, ask if it can both stay active and protect a vulnerable pawn at the same time.

I'd play Rh1. Your Rh2 still leaves a useful rook, but we can use the rook's flexibility better by tucking it to the first rank, freeing the king and bishop while keeping all options open, a technique called "making luft." From h1, if needed, the rook can swing to the queenside later to support advancing our pawns, and your king is finally safe. Takeaway: When the king is slightly exposed, a rook move that clears a flight square while keeping future files open is a strong, flexible regrouping move.

I'd play c4.

Your queen move wasn't bad, but it gave Black an easy chance to push you back with ...b5, gaining space on the queenside. Instead, shield your center pawn on d5 and open the b1-h7 diagonal for your queen while preventing any Black queenside expansions. This focuses on simple, solid development and making your central presence harder to challenge. Takeaway: Protect your center pawns with pawn moves to maintain your space advantage and create better squares for your pieces.

I'd play Qd3.

Your move Qc4 isn't a blunder, but it lets Black push b5 and force your queen to retreat without a plan. Instead, Qd3 does several useful things at once: it protects your weak pawn on c3 and also reinforces your central pawn on d5. This keeps your position solid and allows you to think about connecting your rooks and developing a plan on the next move. Choosing a move that strengthens your structure and defends multiple weaknesses is a great habit to build.

Takeaway: When you have weak pawns, look for a move that defends them while also improving your overall position.

I'd play c4.

This move secures your d5 pawn and stops Black from pushing ...b5 ideas that could challenge your queen later. Right now your queen on c4 looks a bit loose and could be easily harassed by a pawn advance, whereas reinforcing the center with c4 gives you a solid structure. It keeps the position simple and under control, focusing on protecting your assets before starting an offense. Takeaway: Secure your central pawns first when your queen might be driven away by a simple pawn push.

I'd play Re3.

You moved your rook to attack the bishop, but that bishop is tucked away and not doing much right now. Instead, you should use your rook to attack Black's weak pawn on e6, which is only defended by the queen. This makes an immediate threat to win material and also helps defend your queen and f-pawn in the process. It’s a more active and useful way to use your rook.

Takeaway: Attack undefended pawns before going after well-defended pieces.

I'd play R8e7. Right now your knight on d7 is undefended and could be taken by their queen; moving the rook to e7 defends it firmly and also supports your other rook. This keeps your pieces safe and connected, making it much harder for your opponent to win material. Takeaway: when a piece is attacked and undefended, look for a move that defends it while strengthening your position.

I'd play Re3.

Your move Ra3 wasn't a mistake, but Re3 is a stronger idea because it directly attacks the weak pawn on e6 and also makes your queen safer. After that, your rook is well-placed to take the pawn, challenging Black's center and opening lines for your other pieces. This kind of active move, targeting a weakness while improving your piece's safety, is a great habit to build.

Takeaway: When a move attacks an enemy weakness and defends your own pieces at the same time, it's usually a good one.

I'd play Re3. Your Ra3 was active and didn't ruin anything, but Re3 is stronger because it centralizes the rook and immediately probes Black's key weakness: the pawn on e6. After it moves, you threaten to capture it and later use the e-file to increase pressure. This is a practical, forcing move that improves your position while addressing the fact that your rook on b3 currently isn't doing much. Takeaway: When you have two rooks on the board, look to seize an open central file to maximize their power.

I'd play R8e7. Your Rf8 wasn't wrong, but it missed a chance to solidify your position by defending your rook on e5 and your knight on d7 in one move. This puts your pieces on strong, connected squares and leaves White with no easy tactics, allowing you to start coordinating your whole army. Takeaway: Before looking for your own threats, check if one simple move can secure all your undefended pieces.

I'd play R8e7. Instead of putting your rook in the corner, use it to connect your rooks and defend your knight. This keeps your pieces active and safe, and after Qc7 takes the knight, you'll be able to double your rooks on the e-file to press your advantage. Your Rf8 idea missed a chance to secure your position before attacking. Takeaway: When you have a loose piece, defend it actively to keep your whole position strong.

I'd play Qe2.  
First, notice your rook on c2 is hanging, so moving the queen to e2 defends it while also attacking the black pawn on e4. This is a simple, useful idea — defend your pieces while creating a threat of your own. With your rook protected, you keep all your options open instead of trading it off right away.  
Takeaway: Before making a capture, look for a move that defends your threatened pieces and creates a threat in return.

I'd play Qe2.

You chose a practical move that wins the exchange, but instead you can strengthen your position by guarding your rook and threatening a pawn at the same time. Moving your queen to e2 protects your rook on c2, defends your other rook, and puts pressure on Black's e4 pawn. This builds a safe platform for your pieces so you can later use your rooks actively on the open files. Takeaway: When a piece is hanging, see if you can defend it while also creating a useful threat.

I'd play Bxe7.

By taking their bishop on e7, you not only capture a strong piece but also threaten their rook on f8 next move—this is a simple one-two punch that gains you material. Your move Bd2 was safe, but beginners often miss that an undefended piece like your bishop on g5 is only a problem if they can actually take it without a good reply. The key here is to strike first with a capture that develops your initiative.

Takeaway: When your piece is threatened, look for a stronger threat of your own—often a capture that attacks something else.

I'd play Bxe7.

You made a solid defensive move, but here it’s stronger to trade bishops, attacking his rook and freeing your queen’s path. Your bishop on g5 is under attack, and capturing first solves that problem while making his rook on f8 vulnerable. This improves your position by simplifying the board and clearing the way for your heavy pieces to become active.  
Takeaway: When a piece is threatened, look for a trading sequence that also creates a new threat for you.

I'd play Bxe7.

It’s unlikely you overlooked that capturing the bishop also attacks the rook on f8, and it removes a defender of Black's knight. Keeping your bishop actively aimed at the rook discourages Black from moving their knight, because then you could capture the rook. Your move was safe, but at this level you should look for chances to create threats when exchanging pieces. Takeaway: Before retreating a threatened piece, check if it can make one last useful capture that creates a new threat.

I'd play Rxb8.  
Starting with Rxb8 is best because it trades off Black's active rook and then you can move your bishop to c1 to attack their weak pawn on f4. Your move Bd2 defends the c3 pawn, but it misses the chance to improve your rook while creating a threat. By capturing first, you get one piece doing two jobs in a row. Takeaway: When you can trade a less active piece for your opponent's active piece, it’s usually a good deal.

I'd play Qe2.

Your idea to capture the hanging rook was natural, but here it lets Black eliminate your active queen with a discovered attack after their bishop recaptures. Instead, focus on consolidating your position: Qe2 defends both rooks and attacks Black's advanced e4 pawn, fixing your central structure. This keeps your queen safe, keeps material balanced, and prepares to double rooks on the d-file to pressure Black's queen on d5.

Takeaway: Before taking a hanging piece, check if your own active pieces will become vulnerable to a recapture with tempo.

I'd play Rxb8.

Your move Bd2 is solid, but it misses an opportunity to simplify the position favorably. By taking the rook on b8, you force Black to recapture, after which your bishop retreat to c1 attacks the loose pawn on f4 and simultaneously pins their rook. This trades a pair of rooks and grabs a pawn while leaving you with a safer king and active pieces. You correctly spotted your bishop needed to move, but winning material through active trades should come first.

Takeaway: Before retreating a piece, look for forcing exchanges that also create a threat.

I'd play Qc2.

Your move Qe1 protects pieces but misses a chance to attack Black's careless e4 pawn, which right now has no defenders. By moving your queen to c2, you threaten both to capture that pawn and to keep safeguarding your knight and b2 pawn, putting you back on the offensive. This way, you make use of your active pieces to create a concrete threat, fixing the overly defensive habit of just guarding.

Takeaway: When your pieces are well-placed, look for moves that both defend and attack something loose.

I'd play Qc2.

Your move protects the knight but misses that Black's knight on g3 is loose and not defended, so a better idea is to use your queen to threaten their e4 pawn while also protecting your b2 pawn and knight. This puts pressure on Black right away and keeps your pieces safer. After that, you can chase their knight on g3 with your rook and win it if they don't move it.

Takeaway: When you can safely attack an undefended enemy pawn while also defending your own pieces, it’s a strong two-for-one improvement.

I'd play Rxb8. Here you have a concrete opportunity to trade into a bishop endgame where your opponent’s rooks disappear and your bishop becomes stronger than their knight. Taking the rook forces the exchange on b8, then your bishop can retreat to c1, defending your c3 pawn and attacking their advanced f4 pawn. The resulting position is much easier for you to play because you have one active piece against their passive one. Takeaway: When you can trade rooks to reach a favorable minor-piece endgame, especially with connected passed pawns, grab it.

I'd play Qc2.

Your move Qe1 is solid because it defends your knight on c3, but it misses a chance to be active with your queen. By playing Qc2 you attack the unprotected black pawn on e4, which creates immediate pressure while also defending your knight and the b2 pawn. This way your queen is doing useful work instead of just moving back.

Takeaway: When you retreat a piece, aim to keep it active by attacking something or supporting a threat.

I'd play Rc3.  
You had the right idea to defend your attacked pawn, but doing that with the rook blocks your king's path to support it. Instead, move your king towards the center with Kc3—it defends your pawn and clears the way for your rooks to become active. Let your pieces work together by keeping your king safe and central.  
Takeaway: When a pawn is threatened, first consider moving your king to defend it if the square is safe.

I'd play Kf4.  
Kh4 is okay because it protects your pawns, but Kf4 is a bit better because it also attacks the e5 pawn and puts your king in a more central, active position. From f4, your king can later advance to e4 or support pushing your pawns.  
Takeaway: Choose king moves that guard pawns AND move toward the center.

I'd play Rc3.

You defended the pawn, which is natural, but here it allows Black's rook to double on the e-file and create pressure against your king. Instead, seize your chance to be active — your rook on f3 is doing little, so move it toward Black's vulnerable kingside pawns. Rook lifts like Rg3 or Rg1 are powerful because they create threats and can double on files to support a future invasion. Look for ways to attack weak enemy pawns rather than just defending your own.

Takeaway: When you have the initiative, prioritize creating threats over passive defense.

I'd play a4.

Your move Kh4 is safe, but it misses the chance to seize space on the queenside and create a more dangerous passed pawn. By pushing a4, you force Black's king to respond from a distance while your own king stays active enough to protect your key pawns. This little pawn move creates a threat to advance further, pushing Black's weaknesses forward and giving you a clearer target for your king to attack in the endgame.

Takeaway: Use pawn moves to claim space and create a decisive passed pawn before activating your king.

I'd play Ra8.  
Your idea of placing a rook on a useful file is good, but right now your bishop on a7 is loose and needs protection — by moving your rook to a8 you both defend it and attack it, gaining time for your turn. This creates a simple, safe plan: defend your weak pieces first, then look for your own threats.  
Takeaway: Always check which of your pieces are unprotected before moving.

I'd play Rg1. Your move Rc3 defended the pawn but passed on a chance to create direct counterplay by attacking Black's shaky pawn on g6. Takeaway: Look for checks that also improve your piece to a safer or more active square.

I'd play g3.  

Your check gives the black queen a safe square on d4 where it's centralized and defends the pawn, but instead you can immediately attack his advanced pawn. After g3, if he moves his attacked pawn, you can capture it and open the g-file for your rook, creating threats against his king. This is a practical move that takes the initiative and fights for the center of the board.  

Takeaway: When your opponent has an advanced, unprotected pawn, look for a chance to attack it and create openings for your pieces.

I'd play Bh6.  
Let's look at your move, fxe3—it opens the g-file for your rook but immediately hands your opponent's queen a free pawn with Qxh7. Instead, develop your bishop to h6, which defends your vulnerable f4 pawn and puts it on a strong diagonal. This keeps your position solid and prevents that sniper shot on h7 in one go.  
Takeaway: Use one move to defend a weakness and develop a piece toward the center.

I'd play Bh6.

Your move captured a pawn but left your f4-pawn undefended, and your opponent can jump in with Qxh7 next. Instead, develop your bishop to h6 where it does two useful things: it defends that f4-pawn and also prepares to bring your queen safely to g8 after they take on h7, gaining time to finish development. This is a great example of solving two problems with one move while making your position more solid.

Takeaway: When you have an undefended pawn, look for developing moves that also protect it.

I'd play Qa1+.

This good check puts your queen on a safe square where it also defends the rook on e1, and it challenges Black's loose king. Your move Qb2+ was a reasonable check, but Qa1+ accomplishes similar ideas more efficiently by connecting your rooks and forcing Black to block or move the king, after which you can look for forks or pressure on the queenside. It highlights the importance of putting your pieces on squares that serve multiple purposes.

Takeaway: When choosing a check, pick the one that also improves your piece's activity and safety.

I'd play Bh6.  
Your instinct to trade pawns is natural, but right now your f-pawn is undefended, and capturing opens your king's position too early. Instead, develop your bishop to h6, which firmly protects that f4 pawn while challenging White's dominating queen. This keeps your structure solid and allows you to connect your rooks next, fixing the key problem that your pieces aren't coordinating.  
Takeaway: When you have an undefended, advanced pawn, prioritize securing it with pieces over forcing trades that expose your king.

I'd play Qa2.  
Your pawn-to-g6 idea is fine but attacks a piece he can easily defend. Instead, challenge his bishop and go after his unprotected a3 pawn in a way that also ties his pieces down defending each other. This creates more lasting pressure and keeps your queen active.  

Takeaway: When an enemy pawn is weak, improve your attacker's position before taking it.

I'd play Qa2.  

You moved a pawn to attack their f5 pawn, but that lets their queen escape to safety. Instead, putting your queen on a2 attacks two weak white pieces at once: their undefended pawn on a3 and their bishop on d2.  

This creates a simple threat to win material on the next move, and it also defends your f7 pawn against any future checks.  

Takeaway: When you have the chance, attack two of your opponent's pieces at the same time to create easy threats.

I'd play Qa2.

Your pawn push g6 is fine tactically, but playing Qa2 is more instructive because it immediately targets two of White's loose pieces—the bishop on d2 and the knight on e4—forcing them to react. This way, you start improving your queen while keeping pressure and creating future threats, like capturing the pawn on a3, in one purposeful move. It's a flexible, improving move typical of strong positional play.

Takeaway: When the opponent has undefended pieces, use your queen to attack multiple weaknesses at once.

I'd play c4.

You chose to capture a pawn, but right now we have a chance to kick the black queen first and gain space. By pushing your pawn to c4, you attack her queen and force her to move, giving us time to then double rooks on the e-file with full control. Look for moves that improve your position by threatening pieces before grabbing material.  
Takeaway: When your opponent's queen is exposed, use a pawn to chase it away and claim the center.

I'd play c4.

What I like about c4 is that before rushing to capture on f6, you can disrupt their queen’s good square first, gaining time. The move hits her queen on b5, and when she moves, you're then ready to use your rooks and keep solid control of the center. Your idea of capturing on f6 isn't wrong — it’s a solid move — but c4 is more forcing and improves your position before making that capture. This way, you are dictating the flow of the game.

Takeaway: When you have a tempting capture, first check if you can chase an enemy piece to a worse square with tempo.

I'd play Rxe5.  
Capturing with the rook is safe and strong here because it attacks the knight on e7, defends your queen on h5, and leaves your knight ready to leap into the attack. Your move wasn't bad, but taking with the rook connects your rooks and keeps all your pieces active, which is a good habit to build. Think about using your heavy pieces to control the center and defend each other.  
Takeaway: When you can capture a central piece, consider which capturing move improves your position by activating your other pieces.

I'd play Rxe5.  
You naturally wanted to take the loose bishop, and your move was fine, but by capturing with the rook you also defend your queen, which the knight move left hanging temporarily. This keeps your queen powerfully placed on h5, puts your rook in play on a strong central square, and keeps an eye on the black knight on e7. Use your active pieces to build pressure, not just trade pieces.  
Takeaway: When capturing a piece, consider which of your pieces you want to become more active afterward.

I'd play c4.

Your move took a free pawn, but it also exposed your queen to pins and missed a chance to gain the initiative. Pushing c4 attacks the queen and forces it to move, which gains time to then double rooks on the e-file with Rbe1, putting strong pressure on their position. At this level, practicing moves that attack pieces while improving your own coordination is a winning habit.  
Takeaway: When you can attack an opponent's piece and simultaneously develop your own position, that's often stronger than grabbing a loose pawn.

I'd play Nxe5.  
That was a strong practical choice—you immediately remove the bishop while attacking the queen and putting pressure on d3, forcing Black to react instead of developing freely. Your idea to invite ...Qf5, which our queen can simply capture for a decisive double attack, turns the material win into a devastating initiative. Keep trusting these forcing moves that create multiple threats at once.  
Takeaway: When you win a piece, look for follow-up threats that prevent your opponent from simply recapturing.

I'd play Qc8.

You saw that the white queen on c3 was undefended, so you tried to attack it with Rc8, but that allows a surprising knight capture of your queen on d8. Instead, place your own queen on c8, attacking the white queen right away while also defending your rook on a8. This way, you force a queen trade that leaves you safe and equal. Takeaway: When you see a chance to attack an enemy queen, make sure your move isn’t leaving your own queen hanging in return.

I'd play Qc8. While your idea to attack the queen with Rc8 is natural, it leaves your own queen as a target after White’s knight takes our rook on f8. I'd play Bd6. This good developing move immediately attacks the white rook on e5, forcing it to retreat and giving you the initiative. Takeaway: When you have a tempting capture, first look for a developing move that creates a threat.

I'd play Bd6.

Instead of grabbing their pawn, you can attack their rook while defending two of your own pieces. This forces them to move the rook away so you can develop your bishop to a great square next. I'd play Bd6.

I'd play Rxa2.

I'd play Qc8.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s queen is loose, look for ways to attack it actively rather than reacting to other threats.

I'd play Rxa2.  
This picks up a free pawn while also attacking their g-pawn, forcing White to waste a move defending it instead of increasing their pressure. Your move Rd8 allowed White's rook to keep dominating the 7th rank; grabbing material is the simplest way to turn the game around here.  
Takeaway: When you have a safe opportunity, capture free pawns to convert a passive position into a material advantage.

I'd play Rxa2. Your move defends your a-pawn but lets White keep a strong centralized knight — instead, grab their vulnerable a-pawn and increase your rook’s activity, threatening to swing to the kingside later to attack their g-pawn. I'd play Kxe7.  
Instead of taking with your queen, use your king—it gets your king to safety while defending your queen and bishop at the same time. After Qxe7, White's queen can check you, but your king simply moves to d7, staying connected with your pieces. This shows how a king can sometimes be an active defender in the middle game.  
Takeaway: When you can safely capture with your king to centralize and defend pieces, it's often stronger than using your queen.

I'd play Kxe7.

You used your queen to take the unprotected bishop, which is natural, but it blocks your own rook's defense of your queen. Instead, capture with your king; it safely removes the bishop, defends your queen, and unpins your own bishop on f7. After Qe5+ you can simply step your king to d7, keeping everything protected and activating your rook.

Takeaway: When a piece is undefended, consider if a quieter capture with your king solves more problems than using your queen.

I'd play Qxf3. Instead of moving the knight away, grab that free pawn on f3 — it's loose and you can take it safely. After capturing, you're threatening the pawn on d3 and adding pressure to f2, which ties White's pieces down. This turns a quiet move into a direct attack on the opponent's weakened position. Takeaway: Always look for undefended pieces before making a developing move.

I'd play Kxe7.

Instead of bringing your queen out into danger, you can take the bishop with your king — it's safe because White's queen can check you, but you simply move to d7 and then your king is active and your queen stays protected on d8. This solves the loose bishop on e7 without exposing your queen, which is often targeted in these positions. It’s a chance to practice king activity even with major pieces on the board, something that’s important at your level to see.

Takeaway: When you can safely capture with your king to activate it and keep your queen safe, that’s often better than automatically using your queen for the capture.

I'd play Qxd4. Capturing with your queen instead of your pawn attacks the enemy queen right away, forcing a trade that makes the position much simpler and safer. After you trade queens, you're left with no attack to worry about and you can steadily improve your rooks. Your pawn move allowed a rook to attack your h-pawn, making you defend immediately. Takeaway: Before taking a piece, see if capturing with a more powerful piece gives you extra threats.

I'd play Qxd4.

You captured with the pawn, which is natural, but with the queen you win a piece because their bishop isn't defended and you then attack their queen. If they take back, you recapture with your pawn and you've still won the bishop for free, leaving you a strong central pawn and a safer king. Always look for a chance to use your most powerful piece to grab unprotected material.

Takeaway: Before taking with a pawn, check if a higher-value piece can capture first to win material.

I'd play Qxd4.

With your pawn on e3 you removed their bishop, but you passed up an opportunity to develop with tempo and actually put their queen into an awkward pin. Capturing with your queen attacks their hanging queen and also hits their central pawns, making it harder for them to catch up in development while you begin taking over the center. The idea is to follow up with cxd5, piling more pressure on their queen and opening lines for your rooks.

Takeaway: When a piece is hanging, see if a more active recapture also develops a piece and creates a threat.

I'd play Qxf3. Your move hurried a knight to attack the queen, but the pawn on f3 is hanging and taking it opens up White's defenses. For an intermediate player, this teaches the important lesson of grabbing loose pawns and using your queen's power before committing your minor pieces. Takeaway: Look for undefended pieces before making a preparatory move.

I'd play Qxf3. Once we capture the f3 pawn, it's no longer protecting the e4 pawn, which becomes a serious weakness — your next threat is simply Qxd3 winning a second pawn, as the d3 pawn is pinned. This direct, concrete win highlights why your move Na5 was slow: knights on the rim are often dim, but here you missed a chance to immediately exploit White's overextended center. Takeaway: When the opponent has loose pawns in front of their king, look for captures that remove defenders and create multiple undefended targets.

I'd play Rg3.  
Instead of moving your king, your rook attacks the black queen and forces her to move away, gaining you time while also protecting your pawns on c3 and h3. After the queen moves, you can follow up by moving your bishop to g2 next turn, developing your last piece into a strong position.  
Takeaway: Use your moves to create threats that improve your position and restrict your opponent's options.

I'd play Kxf6.

Your move gxf6 gives up your strong center pawn on e5 for no reason, letting the queen take it next. Instead, capture the bishop with your king — that way you keep your great pawn on e5, protect your other pawn on e6, and your king is perfectly safe there. This keeps your position solid and denies White any free targets. Takeaway: When you can capture with either the king or a pawn, consider which recapture better maintains your important pawn structure.

I'd play Kxf6.

Your pawn capture wasn't wrong, but taking with the king is a much stronger idea. It draws your king towards the center for safety and keeps your pawn structure solid to protect your own bishop and pawns. This lets you avoid creating any weaknesses around your king while you coordinate your rooks next. The most important move is often the safe one that improves your king.

Takeaway: When you can capture a piece, consider which recapturer keeps your position safest and most solid.

I'd play Rg3.

That move attacks the unprotected black queen right away, forcing her to move and giving us tempo to improve our other pieces next. Even though Kh1 is okay, a strong player at your level should be spotting direct threats like this — it’s active and creates immediate problems for your opponent. After Rg3, you can follow up with ideas like bringing your bishop to g2 to solidify the kingside and keep building pressure.

I'd play Rg3.  
This move challenges Black's queen and forces it to move, taking the initiative instead of just moving your king. It also reinforces your pawn on c3, which could be a target later, and opens the idea of doubling rooks on the e-file in the future. Your move Kh1 was too passive—when you have an active piece like your rook, look for ways to use it to put pressure on your opponent.

I'd play Kxf6.  
Your pawn capture wasn't a bad move, but this is a cleaner way to take the bishop and keep your king safer on f6, where it defends both e5 and e6 immediately. This prepares to bring your king toward a more central, active role like heading for f7 next, rather than weakening your pawn shield on the kingside. I'd play h4.

Your move h3 was a bit too cautious — it misses a chance to start some action. Instead, push the h-pawn to attack the black pawn on g5; if they take it, you can swing your rook on a7 over to d1 with a nice open file. I'd play Qxd2+.

Taking the queen first simplifies the position favorably for you because it forces the white king to recapture and reveals an attack on the now-undefended bishop on e6. Your move Bxe6 was also fine, but grabbing the queen first transforms the position into a clear material-up endgame you can easily convert. This move sequence trains you to look for forcing exchanges that both win material and improve your piece activity.

I'd play Qxd2+.

Taking the queen first solves the immediate problem of your bishop being attacked, and it also wins the queen, which is far more valuable than simply trading bishops. After the king recaptures, you can still capture the bishop on e6 on the next move, coming out a full queen ahead. The key is to see that the check forces the king to move and leaves no time for White to save both pieces.

Takeaway: When you can win material, capture the more valuable piece first—don’t give your opponent a chance to defend it.

I'd play h4. Right now the black pawn on g5 is a target, and h4 immediately attacks it, creating pressure while also making useful luft for your king. Your move h3 missed the chance to grab the initiative by starting an attack on that weakened pawn chain. After h4, if they take, you can swing a rook to d1 with much more influence in the center and on the seventh rank. Takeaway: When your opponent has a flank pawn majority, look to attack it before it can advance and cramp your position.

I'd play h4.

Instead of playing a waiting move like h3, look ahead to win a pawn: after h4 attacks the pawn on g5, Black can't let it be taken for free, and if they capture hxg4 you can move your rook to d1, picking up the unprotected pawn on b6 a few moves later. This turns your rook on a7 from a passive defender into an active attacker. Takeaway: When you're a pawn up, look for forcing moves that attack enemy pawns to create more weaknesses and convert your advantage.

I'd play Bg7.

You moved the king to defend the h-pawn, but we should think about protecting our most advanced pawn on e5, which is key to controlling the center. Moving the bishop to g7 does exactly that — it defends the pawn and blocks the white rook's view toward your king, making your position safer. It’s a solid move that strengthens your position by safeguarding your central outpost, something beginners should always look for.  
Takeaway: When you can, first defend your advanced pawns, as they give your pieces more space and power.

I'd play Ne7.

Your move Kxh8 was reasonable because you won a bishop, but it let your king get chased. Instead, try Ne7, which attacks the bishop on g6 and also guards your d5 pawn. This makes your position safer, as you're developing a piece and protecting something important at the same time.

Takeaway: When you have a chance to capture, first check if you can attack or defend another piece to improve your position.

I'd play Bg7.

You're correctly looking to get your king away from any potential checks, but putting it on h8 takes it out of the fight and allows White to immediately double rooks on the f-file with tempo, tying yours down to defense. Moving the bishop to g7 is more flexible; it steps back to bolster your key e5 pawn, keeps your king's options open, and prevents White from easily activating their rooks. This way you maintain a solid grip on the center while improving your worst-placed piece.

Takeaway: When seeking safety, prioritize moves that also improve your coordination and restrict your opponent's counterplay.

I'd play Kf7.

Your move Kh8 is safe — it defends the pawn on h7 — but it misses the chance to connect your rooks and improve your king’s role. With Kf7, you step towards the center and protect your bishop on f6, freeing your rook to move and allowing your other rook to enter the game via the f-file next. This creates a solid, active setup where all your pieces work together, which is exactly the kind of positional improvement an intermediate player should look for. The real lesson is to make your king an active supporter in the middlegame when it’s safe to do so.

Takeaway: Use your king actively to connect your rooks and coordinate your pieces.

I'd play Be8.

Your move of taking the bishop is safe for now, but it misses a chance to keep your king safer and attack his stray bishop on g6. Instead by moving your bishop to e8 you threaten the bishop on g6, while defending your knight on c6 so it's not left hanging. This builds your pieces toward the center and keeps your king tucked away, which is a solid positional improvement you can aim for in similar positions.

Takeaway: Before grabbing loose pieces, look for moves that improve your piece coordination and king safety.

I'd play Be8.

Your move was okay, but capturing the bishop lets White’s queen slip in with a check that costs you time, and it misses a chance to first deal with White’s active pieces. By moving your bishop to e8, you attack the bishop on g6 and defend your knight on c6, consolidating your position. This keeps your king safer and puts pressure on their bishop pair, which is more important than grabbing a trapped piece immediately. Takeaway: Before taking a loose piece, ask if you can first improve your piece coordination and limit your opponent’s options.

I'd play Qxg4. Instead of capturing the bishop immediately, start by taking the undefended pawn on g4 with your queen; that also hits the pawn on e6 and importantly defends your own pawn on h3. I'd play Rxd5.

I'd play exd5.

This builds a straightforward plan around the queen's vulnerability.

I'd play Rxd5.

I'd play Qd4.

I'd play Qxg4.

I'd play Qd4.

Your move Qg5 was fine—it pressures the bishop—but Qd4 is more centralizing and effective right now. I'd play h5. Your idea to capture the pawn was natural, but first move the pawn to h5 to safely support your advanced pawn on g4, because right now it's weak and could be captured. I'd play Kc5. Right now your king is doing less than it could; moving forward to c5 attacks both the pawn on c4 and the knight, putting maximum pressure on Black. I'd play Kc5. Instead of grabbing the pawn, you can step forward and attack both the knight and the pawn. I'd play Kc5.

I'd play h5.  
Your move took a free pawn but weakened the defense of your g-pawn, letting White's queen swoop in with check. Instead, h5 quietly defends that pawn and keeps your position solid, so you can later capture on a2 without allowing a dangerous counterattack.  
Takeaway: When you have an advanced pawn like g4, make sure it's secure before grabbing material elsewhere.

I'd play Rd1.  
Your move Qg5 is fine because it threatens the bishop and pressures g6, but right now we're fighting for central control and need to bring the last undeveloped piece into play. I’d play a5.

Your idea of attacking the queen is good, but moving the rook first misses a chance to gain space and force Black’s queen to a less active square. By pushing a5 first, you directly threaten the queen while also preparing a6 to drive her further back, which gives your rook more options next move. This keeps your pieces coordinated and seizes the initiative on the queenside. Takeaway: When you can harass an enemy queen with a pawn push that improves your own structure, do it before committing your heavy pieces.

I'd play Rd2.

Your knight move attacks their knight, but right now your queen and c-pawn are undefended, making it tricky to calculate safely. Simply bringing your rook to the second rank neatly protects both of those pieces at once, making your position solid and freeing your queen to move next turn. This is a great habit: when you see loose pieces, find a safe, simple way to cover them before looking for fancy attacks.

Takeaway: Secure your undefended pieces before starting a counterattack.

I'd play Kg1.

Your move Nd5 isn't bad, but it trades pieces when your king is still a bit exposed on h2; the simple idea is to finish castling by hand. By moving your king to g1, you tuck it safely behind your pawns, connect your rooks, and free your rook on d1 to move anywhere. Your queen is well-placed, so you can then calmly play f3 next to support your center and keep a strong position. Takeaway: Before starting an attack, make sure your own king is secure.

I'd play Bxc6.

Your move was fine, but you missed a chance to weaken Black's queen and open lines for your rook on a1. Capturing that pawn attacks their queen and also defends your e4 pawn, making your center more secure. Beginners often forget to look for captures that create multiple threats like this.

Takeaway: When your bishop is well-placed, check if capturing a pawn also attacks their queen and defends your own pieces.

I'd play Qb6.

While your move Nd5 is a tempting attack on their knight, it trades off your active knight and removes pressure from the black pawn center. The more instructive idea is to use your centralized queen actively, attacking the weak pawns on a6, b5, and d6 all at once—this creates real weaknesses, keeps your pieces on the board, and seizes the initiative. An advanced player should see that before trading, you can first improve your position with a multi-purpose move that creates threats your opponent must address. You threaten to win a pawn while also preparing a check on c5 that could win the rook on e6.

Takeaway: Before trading your active pieces, look for a move that increases your pressure, especially one that attacks multiple weaknesses at once.

I'd play Bxc6.

Your move was safe, but capturing that pawn immediately creates two threats: hitting the queen and attacking the b7 pawn, while defending your own e4 pawn by removing a key attacker. At this level, you want to seize chances to create double attacks. Instead of just moving the bishop to safety, you make Black deal with a capture that improves your pawn structure and gives you activity. Takeaway: When your piece is attacked, look for captures that create immediate threats of your own.

I'd play Qe7.

Your move gives a check but lets White trade queens early. At your level, you want to use your queen actively and keep attacking possibilities. Moving to e7 attacks White's pawn on c5, defends your e4 pawn, and leaves the queen ready to join your rooks later. It's superior because it develops your queen to a strong central square and creates a concrete threat instead of just reacting.

Takeaway: Before trading queens, ask if your queen can first do something useful like attacking a weak pawn or supporting your own pawns.

I'd play Qe7.  
This strong queen move immediately targets your opponent's advanced pawn on c5 and solidly defends your e4 pawn, putting pressure while keeping your options flexible. Your idea is to next bring your rook to d8, gaining control of the open file and developing your last inactive piece. The move you chose trades queens, which relieves pressure and gives your opponent an easy game; here, you want to maintain tension and play for the win.  
Takeaway: Look for moves that develop your pieces to active squares while making useful threats before considering simplifications.

I'd play d4.  
Your move Bc4 is solid, but here you have a chance to seize the center and challenge Black's knight, which is their one active piece. By pushing d4 you open lines for your bishop and queen while attacking two black pieces at once — that's a concrete way to turn your space advantage into real pressure. Then if they take, fxg4 leaves you with a strong pawn center and your rook ready to join the fight.  
Takeaway: When you have space, look for central pawn pushes that attack your opponent's developed pieces.
