I'd play Qe7.  
Right now your queen exchange trades away your strong queen and misses that your pawn on c5 is hanging; Qe7 defends that pawn while also preparing to bring your rook to the open d-file with pressure. The move you played lets White simplify too early—here you want to keep queens on and use your better coordination to target White's loose pawns. A good concrete plan is Qe7, then Rd8, eyeing the d2-square for your knight later.  
Takeaway: Before trading a strong piece, ask if you can first improve your position by defending a weakness and centralizing your forces.

I'd play Qg4+.  
Your check let the black king escape to d7, but Qg4+ forces it toward f5 where we can trade queens and simplify into a winning endgame. Beginners often miss that trading queens is a great way to secure a big advantage, especially when up material like we are here. Look for checks that also limit where your opponent's king can go.  
Takeaway: When you're ahead, a forcing move that leads to a queen trade can simplify and secure your win.

I'd play Qg4+.  
You saw a check, but Qb3+ blocks your bishop and lets Black step toward safety with the king. Instead, aim closer to the king and attack the weak pawn on g7 with tempo—it forces Black's queen to block on f5, and you can simply take it because the pawn can't recapture. This trades queens and leaves you with a safer king and a solid advantage.  
Takeaway: Choose checks that also threaten another target, making your opponent's reply awkward.

I'd play Qg4+. Your move gave a check but allowed the king to escape to d7, losing the initiative. Qg4+ forces the black queen to block on f5, and after you take it, you gain a material advantage and simplify into a winning endgame because their king is so exposed. Instead of just checking, focus on checks that force a reply that wins you something concrete. Takeaway: Look for checks that limit your opponent's options and lead to a profitable trade or capture.

I'd play Qe6.

Your move kept queens on and defended the knight, but Qe6 does that while also attacking the white queen and freeing the f-pawn to support your center. It’s a simple, forcing move that trades queens and simplifies the position, which is often a good idea when you’re up material. The immediate threat is just taking the queen, so White will likely have to respond to it.

Takeaway: When a piece is hanging, look for moves that defend it while making a useful threat of your own.

I'd play Qe6.  
Your move was okay because it attacked their queen, but Qe6 does that while also defending your knight on c6 and your pawn on e4 in one go, making everything solid. This way, if they trade queens, you recapture and your pawn structure stays strong, leaving their king more exposed. Focusing on defending your hanging pieces before starting your own attack is a key habit at this level.  
Takeaway: When you attack, make sure your own pieces are safe first—one move that both challenges and defends is often best.

I'd play Qe6. The immediate idea is simple: attack their centralized queen while also defending your knight on c6 and the e4 pawn, so if they swap queens you comfortably recapture and keep a solid structure. This highlights a common theme — when your opponent has a queen that's a bit too daring, first restrain the temptation to attack elsewhere and just force a trade to ease your position. By kicking their queen first, you take the initiative away before starting your own plans. Takeaway: When their queen is overextended, look for ways to challenge it while reinforcing your own pieces.

I'd play Nxc3.

Your rook move missed a chance to settle the tension before claiming the file. Taking their knight now removes their active piece, defends your weak pawn on a4, and makes your rook on e4 safe—fixing two of your undefended pieces in one move. After that, you can look to push your pawn to a5 and trap their rook.

Takeaway: When you have several loose pieces, see if one move can solve multiple problems at once.

I'd play Nxc3.  
That move trades knights, uncovering your undefended rook on e4, and also defends the a‑pawn. Instead of moving your rook back to e1, which lets Black trade rooks and take your knight, you want to clear the c3-square to protect your rook and keep your extra piece. Just take the knight first and see what Black does next—your rook is safe now.  
Takeaway: When a piece is hanging, look for a move that defends it while also accomplishing something else, like trading or developing.

I'd play Nxc3.

This consolidates your position before looking for activity, a good habit when you have multiple pieces under threat. Takeaway: When your pieces are undefended, prioritize a move that develops while removing an attacker of those pieces.

Takeaway: Challenge your opponent’s advanced pieces directly when you can, before settling on a quieter development.

Takeaway: Develop your pieces to good squares before making a trade to get the most out of your moves.

It turns your rooks into active players in the game rather than just defenders. Takeaway: Activate a rook toward a weak opponent pawn to start a concrete attack.

I'd play Rb1. This starts making use of your rook on a1 by attacking the weak pawn on b7, and it unpins your bishop on c1 so your minor pieces are safer. Your move Re1 defends your e-pawn, but that pawn is already well-protected, so you can instead start threatening Black on the queenside where they are less developed. Focus first on developing your rooks toward open files and creating threats. Takeaway: When your center is solid, look to activate your rooks by targeting weak enemy pawns.

I'd play Qd7.

Your move captured the pawn, which is often a good instinct, but here it opens the center when your king is still in the middle. Instead, let's focus on getting your king safe. Qd7 connects your rooks and prepares to castle queenside, which solves your main problem of king safety. It also helps defend your queenside pawns.

Takeaway: In the opening, prioritize getting your king safe and your pieces coordinated before starting trades in the center.

I'd play Qd7.  
Your move bxc4 opens the b-file, but it lets White trade pawns and target your hanging a7 pawn after dxc4. Instead, connect your queen to defend b5 and a7 in one move, keeping your pawn structure solid for now. This leaves you flexible to handle any pawn exchanges on the b-file while developing your queen to a useful square.  
Takeaway: When you have multiple weak pawns, one move that defends them all often saves time and keeps your position secure.

I'd play c5.  
Your move Bg4 was a bit early because White can simply push h3 and make your bishop retreat, losing time. Instead, use your turn to attack White's strong center pawn with c5 right away, challenging their control of the board. This follows a key beginner principle: strike back in the center before completing your own development.  
Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong center, look for a pawn move to attack it immediately.

I'd play g6.

Your bxc4 move wasn’t a mistake, but it gives up good central tension by taking a pawn that wasn't threatening anything yet. Instead, g6 sets up a flexible position, getting your king safer and preparing to bring your bishop to g7 where it will put more pressure on White's center. This keeps your strong pawn structure intact and avoids committing your pieces too early. Takeaway: When your position is solid, prioritize improving your king safety and piece coordination over trading pawns in the center.

I'd play c5. Your Bg4 isn't bad, but it gives White the chance to play h3 and make you move the bishop again, losing a bit of time. Instead, by pushing c5 you immediately challenge White's strong central pawn. If they take, you can recapture with your bishop and develop it strongly to h3 next, gaining active play. Takeaway: Counter in the center first to limit your opponent's options before committing your minor pieces.

I'd play c5.

Instead of moving your bishop out right away, challenge White's center immediately with your c-pawn. The move Bg4 allows White to play h3 and kick your bishop, but c5 fights for the initiative by attacking their pawn on d4. This is the kind of principled move — striking in the center before completing kingside development — that is both sound and teaches you to seize your share of the board.

Takeaway: When your opponent hasn't castled yet, look for ways to disrupt their central pawns first.

I'd play h6.  
Your move Re8 was fine for safety, but here you have a chance to be more active by kicking that bishop that's pinning your knight; that bishop has no defender, and making it move now stops it from bothering you later. After the bishop retreats, you can follow up with Re8 anyway to complete your development.  
Takeaway: When a piece is bothering you, look for a simple pawn push to chase it away before it becomes a bigger problem.

I'd play h6.  
Your move Re8 defends your bishop and keeps castling short, but you missed the chance to gain time by kicking White's bishop. Playing h6 attacks the bishop and forces it to decide where to go, giving you more control of the board before you commit your rooks. After the bishop moves, your game is easier to develop.  
Takeaway: When an opponent's piece is loose, make a useful attacking move to force it away before finishing your own development.

I'd play h6.

Your move Re8 is solid, but it doesn't address the pressure your knights are under; the bishop on g5 limits your knights and stops your kingside development. By playing h6 first, you force White to make a decision with that bishop, clearing the way so you can develop your knight to d7 next without it being pinned and attacked. This simple one-move threat is a small but useful tempo-gainer that solves a key problem in your position.

Takeaway: Before moving your rook to a safe square, first press an annoying enemy piece to gain time for your development.

I'd play c6. That move protects both your b5 and d5 pawns at once, which is important because they're both currently unprotected and might be targets for your opponent. Your move g6 doesn't address those loose pieces, and here we want to fix structural weaknesses first before making moves that don't help our pieces. By pushing c6, you make your center solid and free your other pieces from having to babysit pawns.  
Takeaway: When you have multiple undefended pawns, look for a single move that safeguards them all and strengthens your position.

I'd play Qd7.  
Your move g6 wasn't bad, but with your queen you can defend two things at once — the c7 pawn and the b5 pawn — which frees your other pieces to get active. From d7, your queen also eyes the queenside and connects your rooks, preparing a plan like ...c6 to firm up your center. The idea is to use your strongest piece to solidify your position before launching your own play.  
Takeaway: Use your queen to defend multiple weaknesses in one move before starting your own plans.

I'd play c6.  
Your idea to prepare safety with g6 is sensible, but here it spends a tempo and slightly weakens your kingside dark squares without addressing the real question: how to secure your center and free your pieces. c6 solidly defends your d5 pawn, supports your b5 pawn, and opens the possibility of expanding with ...b4 to challenge White’s central knight after it moves to c3. This small move unites your position and prepares a queenside initiative much more effectively.  
Takeaway: When your structure lacks clear weaknesses, a consolidating pawn move that strengthens your center and prepares a plan is often more important than a purely precautionary kingside pawn move.

I'd play b5.
